key_insight: A self-supervised mask-aware Transformer can learn fault-tolerant force estimation from multi-channel FBG sensors by explicitly handling missing channels and degrading gracefully under fiber fractures.
engineering_relevance: This enables safer, more practical minimally invasive surgical robotics by reducing calibration burden and maintaining force sensing when some optical channels fail.
method: Pretrain the Transformer with masked-channel reconstruction on unlabeled sensor streams.
Fine-tune for force regression using both clean and corrupted views with a dynamic corruption curriculum.
Add a heteroscedastic uncertainty head to predict per-axis confidence in one forward pass.
evidence_signal: On an 8-channel catheter-scale FBG dataset, the model achieved 0.0066 N RMSE nominally and 0.0126 N with severe 4-channel failure, outperforming a 255-model bank that reached 0.0154 N under 4-channel loss.
watch_item: Performance likely depends on representative corruption patterns during pretraining and fine-tuning, so unseen failure modes or poor mask modeling could still reduce robustness.
paper_title: Self-Supervised Mask-Aware Transformers for Fault-Tolerant FBG Force Sensing in Minimally Invasive Surgical Robotics
